original_abstract: Many peptide hormones adopt α-helical structures upon receptor binding, and the ability to detect these molecules with high sensitivity is critical for disease management. While de novo protein design has successfully produced high-affinity binders for structured proteins, designing interactions for short, flexible peptides with helical propensity has remained a significant challenge. Here, we present parametric generation and deep learning-based strategies to overcome this limitation. By extending RFdiffusion to accommodate flexible targets and implementing a "partial diffusion" process—which refines structural models through successive noising and denoising—we generated picomolar-affinity binders for several helical peptide targets. These binders were achieved both by optimizing existing designs and through completely de novo generation from random noise, without requiring experimental maturation. We demonstrate that these RFdiffusion-optimized designs enable the enrichment and mass spectrometry-based detection of parathyroid hormone (PTH) and glucagon from complex samples, and can be integrated into bioluminescent protein biosensors. This capacity to design binders for conformationally variable targets and to refine both natural and synthetic proteins through partial diffusion offers a powerful new toolset for proteomics and diagnostics.
humanized_abstract: Several peptide hormones are α-helical upon receptor binding, which demands sensitive detection for pathophysiological diagnosis. Designing high affinity binding for folded proteins is now common place in de novo design, but designing binding to short, flexible peptides that only contain random coils connected by helical motifs remain a significant challenge. We here describe methods to overcome the latter challenge through employing parametric modeling and deep learning. We applied an extension of RFdiffusion (RFdiffusionPRO) to flexible targets that combines the partial diffusion method (PDM) comprising successively noising and denoising the molecule, to design picomolar affinity binders to a few examples of these helical motifs both from the optimization of known binders as well as from pure de novo design originating from random noise, requiring no further maturation. We show that the designs facilitated enrichment and sensitive detection of parathyroid hormone (PTH) and glucagon by mass spectrometry (MS), while they can also be adapted to be used in bioluminescent protein based protein-protein biosensors. In summary, designs binders to conformationally variable peptides and refines natural as well as synthetic proteins using the partial diffusion method.

We show that the designs facilitated enrichment and sensitive detection of parathyroid hormone (PTH) and glucagon by mass spectrometry (MS), while they can also be adapted to be used in bioluminescent protein based protein-protein biosensors. In summary, designs binders to conformationally variable peptides and refines natural as well as synthetic proteins using the partial diffusion method.
